topic.what: Clinical pilot study evaluating the efficacy of Amlapitta Mishran — a classical Ayurvedic polyherbal formulation — in patients diagnosed with Amlapitta, the Ayurvedic disease category corresponding to acid gastritis and hyperacidity with pitta-dosha excess.
topic.how: Case-series / pilot clinical design; patients diagnosed with Amlapitta per classical criteria received Amlapitta Mishran for a defined treatment course; outcomes measured on Ayurvedic symptom scales (daha, amlaudgara, hrit-kantha-daha) and standard gastric symptom scores.
topic.why_matters: Amlapitta is one of the most prevalent conditions managed in Ayurvedic practice and maps partially onto functional dyspepsia and acid gastritis in biomedicine; demonstrating clinical signal for a classical formula validates the disease ontology and opens a translational bridge between Ayurvedic and biomedical gastroenterology.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in classical Amlapitta symptoms: burning sensation (daha), acid regurgitation (amlaodgara), throat-chest burning (hrit-kantha-daha)
characteristics.value: Clinically meaningful reduction in symptom severity in majority of patients
characteristics.evidence: Within-group pre-post comparison in case series
characteristics.context: No control arm; case series design limits causal inference

characteristics.value: No serious adverse events reported; good tolerability
characteristics.context: Small sample; long-term safety not assessed

characteristics.what_it_measures: Clarity of correspondence between Amlapitta criteria and biomedical gastric diagnoses
characteristics.value: Partial overlap with functional dyspepsia and acid gastritis; not equivalent to endoscopic gastritis
characteristics.evidence: Discussed in paper introduction and methods
characteristics.context: Conceptual bridge rather than validated equivalence

good_for: Understanding Ayurvedic disease ontology for gastric conditions (Amlapitta as a distinct diagnostic category)
Generating hypotheses for RCT design comparing Amlapitta Mishran to standard antacid/PPI therapy
Informing patient stratification in integrative gastroenterology studies
Demonstrating feasibility of Ayurvedic clinical methodology for gastric symptom assessment
not_for: Definitive efficacy conclusions — no control group
H. pylori or structural gastritis management
Dosing guidance for large-scale clinical use without further validation
requires: RCT with active comparator (antacid or PPI) and endoscopic confirmation
Validated cross-walk between Amlapitta symptom scale and Rome IV / GSRS instruments
Phytochemical characterisation of Amlapitta Mishran batch used

novelty: Provides contemporary clinical pilot data for a classical Ayurvedic formulation specifically indicated for acid gastritis-like syndrome, an underrepresented evidence category
Applies Ayurvedic disease-specific outcome measures (Amlapitta symptom scale) alongside standard gastric symptom tools — a methodological bridging contribution
Highlights pitta-dosha excess as a diagnostic axis that partially maps onto acid hypersecretion and mucosal inflammation, enabling cross-paradigm hypothesis generation
open_problems: No randomised controlled trial confirming efficacy over placebo or active control
Endoscopic or histological correlation with Amlapitta diagnosis not established
Biomedical mechanism of Amlapitta Mishran components not characterised
Sample size too small for subgroup analyses by dosha constitution (prakriti)
Long-term relapse rate after treatment cessation unknown
